families.name: Falconidae
families.description: Falcons are birds of prey in the genus Falco, which includes about 40 species. They are widely distributed on all continents of the world except Antarctica.

families.name: Gruidae
families.description: Cranes are a family, the Gruidae, of large, long-legged, and long-necked birds in the order Gruiformes.

families.name: Laridae
families.description: Gulls are seabirds in the family Laridae. They are most closely related to the terns and skimmers.

families.name: Emberizidae
families.description: Buntings are a family of passerine birds, a group of seed-eating birds with a distinctively shaped bill.

birds.description: The Amur Falcon is a small raptor of the falcon family. It breeds in south-eastern Siberia and Northern China and migrates across India and over the Arabian Sea to winter in Southern Africa. It is a fairly common breeding visitor to forest steppe regions of Mongolia.
birds.identification: Adult males are distinctive with dark grey upperparts, white underparts, chestnut thighs and vent, and white underwing coverts. Females have a grey cap, white face with a dark moustache, and barred underparts.
birds.habitat: Breeds in open forest, forest edges, and steppe with scattered trees. Often found near water bodies during migration.
birds.diet: Feeds primarily on insects, especially termites, dragonflies, and grasshoppers. Also takes small birds and amphibians during breeding season.
birds.breeding: Nests in old tree nests of other birds, often in small colonies. Lays 3-4 eggs. Incubation period is about 28-30 days.
birds.migration: One of the most remarkable migrations of any raptor, travelling from breeding grounds in Mongolia and China to wintering areas in Southern Africa, a journey of over 14,000 km.
birds.threats: Habitat loss and degradation, pesticide use affecting insect prey populations, and hunting during migration.
birds.population: Stable. Global population estimated at 300,000-500,000 breeding pairs.
images: Amur Falcon adult female, Fairly Common Breeding Visitor to Forest Steppe.JPG

birds.description: Pallas's Bunting is a small passerine bird in the bunting family. It breeds across central and eastern Asia, including much of Mongolia, and winters in southern and eastern Asia.
birds.identification: Adult male has a black crown, face, and throat in breeding plumage, with a white stripe behind the eye. Upperparts are chestnut with black streaks. Female and non-breeding males are more buff-brown with streaking.
birds.habitat: Prefers montane shrubland, alpine meadows, and steppe grasslands. Often found near water sources in arid regions.
birds.diet: Feeds on seeds and insects, foraging on the ground in small flocks.
birds.breeding: Nests on or near the ground in dense vegetation. Clutch size is typically 4-5 eggs.
birds.migration: Partially migratory. Northern populations move south in winter, while southern populations may be resident.
birds.threats: Habitat alteration in breeding and wintering grounds. Generally not considered threatened.
birds.population: Common and widespread. Population estimated to be in the millions.
images: Bunting, Pallas's, adult male, Ugii lake, June, 2014 (9).jpg

birds.description: The Demoiselle Crane is the smallest species of crane. It breeds in central Asia, including Mongolia, and winters in Africa and the Indian subcontinent. Known for their spectacular migrations over the Himalayas.
birds.identification: Pale blue-grey body, black neck and breast with a white stripe extending from the eyes down the sides of the neck. They have a distinctive white plume of feathers on the sides of the head.
birds.habitat: Breeds in dry steppe and semi-desert regions, often near water. During migration and winter, found in agricultural areas and wetlands.
birds.diet: Omnivorous, feeding on grains, seeds, insects, small mammals, and reptiles.
birds.breeding: Nests on the ground in open areas. Lays 2 eggs. Both parents incubate and care for the young.
birds.migration: Migrates in large flocks over the Himalayas, reaching altitudes of up to 8,000 meters. Winters in Africa and India.
birds.threats: Habitat loss due to agricultural intensification, hunting along migration routes, and collision with power lines.
birds.population: Stable to decreasing. Global population estimated at 200,000-250,000 individuals.

birds.description: The Relict Gull is a medium-sized gull and one of the rarest gull species in the world. It was first described as a species in 1931 and breeds in isolated colonies in Central Asia, including Mongolia, Kazakhstan, and China.
birds.identification: White head and body with a distinctive dark hood, pale grey wings with black tips, and a red bill and legs. The hood is more extensive in breeding adults.
birds.habitat: Breeds on islands in saline and freshwater lakes in steppe and desert regions. Winters along coastlines and large inland water bodies.
birds.diet: Feeds on fish, aquatic invertebrates, and occasionally takes eggs and chicks of other waterbirds.
birds.breeding: Nests in colonies on islands, laying 2-3 eggs. Highly sensitive to disturbance during breeding season.
birds.migration: Migrates to coastal areas of eastern Asia, including China, Korea, and Japan for winter.
birds.threats: Habitat loss and degradation of breeding lakes, disturbance at colonies, egg collection, and climate change affecting water levels.
birds.population: Decreasing. Global population estimated at 10,000-15,000 mature individuals.

birds.description: The Saker Falcon is a large species of falcon. It breeds from eastern Europe eastwards across Asia to Manchuria. It is a key species in Mongolia, where it holds cultural significance and is used in traditional falconry.
birds.identification: Large falcon with broad wings. Plumage varies from pale brown to dark brown above, with streaked underparts. Dark moustachial stripe on face. In flight, shows distinctive pale underwing coverts.
birds.habitat: Prefers open landscapes including steppe, semi-desert, and agricultural areas. Nests on cliffs, rock outcrops, and sometimes in old tree nests of other large birds.
birds.diet: Feeds primarily on medium-sized mammals such as ground squirrels, voles, and pikas, as well as birds like pigeons and partridges.
birds.breeding: Lays 3-5 eggs. Incubation period is about 30 days. Young fledge at 45-50 days.
birds.migration: Partially migratory. Some populations migrate south in winter, while others are resident year-round.
birds.threats: Major threats include electrocution on power lines, habitat degradation, illegal capture for falconry trade, and pesticide use affecting prey populations.
birds.population: Decreasing. Global population estimated at 6,000-12,000 breeding pairs.

members.name: Dr. S. Gombobaatar
members.title: Founder & Director
members.bio: One of the founders of the Society in 1999. Since then, he has been extensively dealing with activities on bird research, conservation, and international collaborations for the Society. He is also head of the Laboratory of Ornithology at the National University of Mongolia. Gomboo wrote his master thesis on Cranes of Mongolia in 1996 and Ph.D. thesis on Saker Falcon in Mongolia in 2006. He has been supervising and coordinating all projects and research works of the Society, and supervising theses of B.Sc., MSc., Ph.D students at his Laboratory.

members.name: Ch. Uuganbayar MSc.
members.title: Board Member
members.bio: One of the pioneer members of the society. His bachelor and master thesis were written on the diet composition of Sakers in Central Mongolia. Since 2002, Uugan has been working at the Biology Department of Mongolian State University of Agriculture. He successfully organized several birding and ornithological expeditions in Eastern Mongolia.

members.name: D. Usukhjargal MSc.
members.title: Board Member
members.bio: He has been working at Hustai Nuruu National Park as a Takhi biologist since 2003. He is one of the experts on birds in the areas. He completed his master thesis on Reed Deer in Hustai Nuruu National Park. Now he is studying doctorate training at the National University of Mongolia.

members.name: P. Amartuvshin MSc.
members.title: Board Member
members.bio: He is one of the researchers of the Society. Amaraa wrote his master thesis on Impacts of power lines on bird mortality in 2010. He has participated in research projects on Regional Red List of Birds, risk assessments of high power electric lines, and Important Bird Areas surveys. He has been guiding the Society's birding tours since 2009.

members.name: B. Odkhuu MSc.
members.title: Ornithologist
members.bio: Ornithologist at Chinggis Khaan International Airport, working to reduce bird and aircraft strike hazards since 2008. He wrote his bachelor thesis on Saker falcon's sex and age identification and master thesis on biological surveys of upland buzzard. He is interested in studying urban birds and raptors.

members.name: B. Gantulga Dr.
members.title: Researcher
members.bio: Received his bachelor and master degree from the National University of Mongolia. His master thesis was on breeding success of Azure-winged magpie. He has been a member of the Mongolian Ornithological Society since 2005 and is well experienced in field study and birding.

members.name: O. Soronzonbold MSc.
members.title: Young Member
members.bio: One of the young members of the society. He participates in Pallas's fish eagle and Saker surveys, helps organise conferences and workshops. He's interested in studying conservation genetics and biology of birds and wildlife in Mongolia.

members.name: B. Yumjirmaa
members.title: Member
members.bio: Graduated from the National University of Mongolia majoring in Ecology and Nature Conservation. She has been a member of the Society since 2009 and has actively participated in conferences, workshops and birding trips.

members.name: U. Tuvshin
members.title: Researcher
members.bio: Graduated from Eco-Asia Institute's Ecology & Conservation class. His research work includes observation of birds in the Avian Influenza Mongolia Project, Great Bustard's migration, and water birds migration studies.

members.name: E. Unurjargal
members.title: Young Member
members.bio: One of the young members of the society. She graduated from the National University of Mongolia's Ecotourism Management class. She participates in bird watching tours and helps organize conferences and workshops.

blocks.title: Thelonius Monk
blocks.body: You've got to dig it to dig it, you dig?

## Spatiantia astra 

Foeda, medio silva *errandum*: onus formam munere. Mutata bibulis est auxiliare arces etiamnunc verbis virgineo Priamidas illa Thescelus, nam fit locis lucis auras. Exitus hospes gratulor ut pondere [speslimite](http://www.curas.io/figuram); quid habent, Avernales faciente de. Pervenit Ino sonabile supplex cognoscenti vires, Bacchumque errat miserarum venandi dignabere dedisti. Discrimina iuncosaque virgaque tot sine superest [fissus](http://quos.org/sitet.aspx). Non color esset potest non sumit, sed vix arserat. Nisi immo silva tantum pectusque quos pennis quisquam artus!
